claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let's start: **How many children are you looking to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit. I have one child - Alex, who is 17 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Alex.

Since Alex is 17 years old, their eligibility for Child Benefit depends on their current situation. 

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** For example, are they studying A-levels, T-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs up to Level 3, or enrolled on a traineeship in England?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. They left school and aren't studying anything right now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that.

I just need to check one more thing about Alex's situation.

**Is Alex registered with their local careers service, Connexions, or a similar organisation for training or employment opportunities?** This sometimes applies to 16 and 17 year olds who have left education but are registered for certain support services.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex hasn't registered with the careers service or anything like that. They just finished school and haven't signed up for any of those services.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Let me check the eligibility rules to make sure I give you accurate information about Alex's situation.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information I've gathered and the eligibility rules, I now have enough information to make a determination about Alex's eligibility.

Here's what I found:

- Child Benefit normally continues for children aged 16-19 **only if** they stay in approved education or training
- For 16 or 17 year olds who leave education or training, Child Benefit can continue for **20 weeks** if they register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services
- Alex is 17, has left school, is not in approved education or training, and has not registered with a careers service or similar organisation

Let me provide you with my eligibility assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the UK government's Child Benefit rules, I'm afraid **you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why:

**For Alex (aged 17):**
- Child Benefit for children aged 16 or over only continues if the child remains in **approved education or training** (such as A-levels, T-levels, or certain vocational qualifications)
- Alternatively, for 16 and 17 year olds who have left education, Child Benefit can continue for **20 weeks** if they register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services
- Since Alex has left school, is not in approved education or training, and has not registered with a careers service, they do not meet the eligibility criteria

**What could change this?**
If Alex were to:
1. **Return to approved education or training** (e.g., enrol in a college course, apprenticeship qualification, or similar), or
2. **Register with a careers service** (which would allow Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks while they look for work or training)

...then you may become eligible to claim or continue receiving Child Benefit.

I hope this information is helpful. If you have any questions or Alex's circumstances change, please don't hesitate to ask.

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and has left school. For children aged 16 or over, Child Benefit only continues if the child stays in approved education or training. Alternatively, for 16 or 17 year olds who have left education, Child Benefit can continue for 20 weeks if they register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Since Alex is not in approved education or training AND has not registered with a careers service or similar organisation, Alex does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, aged 17, who has left school and is not in approved education or training. Alex has also not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Under Child Benefit rules, children aged 16 or over only qualify if they remain in approved education/training, or (for 16-17 year olds) if they register with a careers service after leaving education. Since neither condition is met, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Alternatively, for 16 or 17 year olds who have left education, Child Benefit can continue for 20 weeks if they register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Since Alex is not in approved education or training AND has not registered with a careers service or similar organisation, Alex does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, aged 17, who has left school and is not in approved education or training. Alex has also not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Under Child Benefit rules, children aged 16 or over only qualify if they remain in approved education/training, or (for 16-17 year olds) if they register with a careers service after leaving education. Since neither condition is met, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
